nodes.title: Define a matrix as a rectangular array
nodes.brief: Recognise a matrix as an ordered rectangular array of numbers or functions, whose entries are its elements.
nodes.quote: A *matrix* is an ordered rectangular array of numbers or functions.
nodes.explanation: A matrix is an ordered rectangular array of numbers or functions; the numbers or functions are called the elements (or entries) of the matrix. The horizontal lines of elements are the rows and the vertical lines are the columns. Matrices give a compact way to record tabular data - for example the notebooks and pens owned by several people - and are denoted by capital letters.
keyMoves: Lay the data out as a rectangular array enclosed in brackets
Read off rows (horizontal) and columns (vertical)
Call each individual entry an element of the matrix
misconceptions: Confusing a matrix (an array) with a single number
Mixing up which direction is a row and which is a column

nodes.title: State the order of a matrix
nodes.brief: Describe a matrix with m rows and n columns as an m x n matrix, with mn elements.
nodes.quote: A matrix having *m* rows and *n* columns is called a matrix of *order*  $m \times n$
nodes.explanation: A matrix having m rows and n columns is called a matrix of order m x n (read 'm by n'). The order tells you the shape of the array; the total number of elements equals mn. For example a 3 x 2 matrix has 3 rows, 2 columns and 6 elements. The element a_ij sits in the i-th row and j-th column.
keyMoves: Count the rows m and the columns n
Write the order as m x n
Find the number of elements as mn
misconceptions: Writing the order as columns x rows instead of rows x columns
Confusing the order with the number of elements

nodes.title: Recognise a square matrix
nodes.brief: Identify a square matrix as one with equal numbers of rows and columns (m = n).
nodes.quote: A matrix in which the number of rows are equal to the number of columns, is said to be a *square matrix*.
nodes.explanation: A matrix in which the number of rows equals the number of columns is a square matrix; an m x n matrix is square when m = n, and is then called a square matrix of order n. The entries a_11, a_22, ..., a_nn form its diagonal. Square matrices are the only ones that can have an identity, powers, and (sometimes) an inverse.
keyMoves: Check whether the number of rows equals the number of columns
If m = n, call it a square matrix of order n
Identify the diagonal entries a_11, a_22, ..., a_nn
misconceptions: Calling any rectangular matrix square
Confusing the diagonal with a whole row or column

nodes.title: Recognise a diagonal matrix
nodes.brief: Identify a diagonal matrix as a square matrix whose non-diagonal elements are all zero.
nodes.quote: is said to be a *diagonal matrix* if all its non diagonal elements are zero
nodes.explanation: A square matrix B = [b_ij] is a diagonal matrix if all its non-diagonal elements are zero, i.e. b_ij = 0 whenever i is not equal to j. Only the entries on the main diagonal may be non-zero. For example, diag(-1, 2) is a 2 x 2 diagonal matrix. Diagonal matrices of the same order multiply commutatively.
keyMoves: Confirm the matrix is square
Check every off-diagonal entry is zero
Allow the diagonal entries to be any values
misconceptions: Requiring the diagonal entries to be equal (that is a scalar matrix)
Calling a non-square matrix diagonal

nodes.title: Recognise a scalar matrix
nodes.brief: Identify a scalar matrix as a diagonal matrix whose diagonal elements are all equal.
nodes.quote: A diagonal matrix is said to be a *scalar matrix* if its diagonal elements are equal
nodes.explanation: A diagonal matrix is a scalar matrix if its diagonal elements are equal: b_ij = 0 when i is not equal to j, and b_ii = k (a constant) when i = j. So a scalar matrix is a constant times the identity. When k = 1 the scalar matrix becomes the identity matrix; every identity matrix is therefore a scalar matrix.
keyMoves: Confirm the matrix is diagonal
Check all the diagonal entries are equal to the same constant k
Recognise it as k times the identity matrix
misconceptions: Allowing unequal diagonal entries in a scalar matrix
Forgetting that the identity is the special scalar matrix with k = 1

nodes.title: Recognise the identity matrix
nodes.brief: Identify the identity matrix as a square matrix with 1s on the diagonal and 0s elsewhere.
nodes.quote: A square matrix in which elements in the diagonal are all 1 and rest are all zero is called an *identity matrix*.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The defining pattern: 1s on the diagonal, 0s everywhere else.
The identity matrix I_n is a square n×n matrix with a_ij = 1 if i = j (diagonal entries) and a_ij = 0 if i ≠ j (off-diagonal entries). The 2×2 identity is [[1,0],[0,1]] and the 3×3 identity is [[1,0,0],[0,1,0],[0,0,1]]. It is NOT the zero matrix — the zero matrix has 0s everywhere including the diagonal.

PART 2 — The key property: IA = AI = A for any same-order square A.
Multiplying any square matrix A (of the same order) by I on either side leaves A unchanged: IA = AI = A. This is why I is called the multiplicative identity — it plays the role of 1 in ordinary number multiplication.

PART 3 — Use I to set up and check inverse problems.
A matrix B is the inverse of A if and only if AB = BA = I. When verifying an inverse, compute the product and check whether you get I — every diagonal entry must be 1 and every off-diagonal entry 0.
keyMoves: Place 1s along the whole diagonal
Set every off-diagonal entry to 0
Use IA = AI = A as the identity property
misconceptions: Confusing the identity matrix with the zero matrix
Forgetting the identity must be square

nodes.title: Recognise the zero (null) matrix
nodes.brief: Identify the zero matrix as one in which all elements are zero.
nodes.quote: A matrix is said to be zero matrix or null matrix if all its elements are zero.
nodes.explanation: A matrix is a zero matrix (or null matrix) if all its elements are zero; it is denoted by O and may have any order. The zero matrix is the additive identity: A + O = O + A = A. Note a product of two non-zero matrices can be the zero matrix, so AB = O does not force A = O or B = O.
keyMoves: Check every entry of the matrix is zero
Denote it O and infer its order from context
Use A + O = A as the additive-identity property
misconceptions: Thinking AB = O forces one of A, B to be the zero matrix
Confusing the zero matrix with the identity matrix

nodes.title: Test two matrices for equality
nodes.brief: Two matrices are equal iff they have the same order and equal corresponding elements.
nodes.quote: each element of A is equal to the corresponding element of B, that is  $a_{ij} = b_{ij}$  for all *i* and *j*.
nodes.explanation: Two matrices A = [a_ij] and B = [b_ij] are equal if (i) they are of the same order and (ii) each element of A equals the corresponding element of B, that is a_ij = b_ij for all i and j. This lets you solve for unknown entries: if two matrices are stated equal, equate corresponding entries and solve the resulting equations.
keyMoves: Check the two matrices have the same order
Equate corresponding entries a_ij = b_ij
Solve the resulting equations for any unknowns
misconceptions: Calling matrices of different orders equal because they share some entries
Equating entries in the wrong positions

nodes.title: Add two matrices of the same order
nodes.brief: Add matrices entrywise; the operation is defined only for matrices of the same order.
nodes.quote: the sum of the two matrices A and B is *defined* as a matrix  $C = [c_{ij}]_{m \times n}$ , where  $c_{ij} = a_{ij} + b_{ij}$
nodes.explanation: If A = [a_ij] and B = [b_ij] are two matrices of the same order m x n, their sum is the matrix C = [c_ij] of order m x n with c_ij = a_ij + b_ij - you add corresponding elements. Addition is defined ONLY when the two matrices have the same order. Matrix addition is commutative and associative, with the zero matrix as identity and -A as the additive inverse of A.
keyMoves: Check both matrices have the same order
Add corresponding entries: c_ij = a_ij + b_ij
Report the sum, of the same order
misconceptions: Trying to add matrices of different orders
Adding only the diagonal or some entries rather than all corresponding ones

nodes.title: Multiply a matrix by a scalar
nodes.brief: Multiply a matrix by a scalar k by multiplying every element by k.
nodes.quote: kA is another matrix which is obtained by multiplying each element of A by the scalar k.
nodes.explanation: If A = [a_ij] is a matrix and k is a scalar, then kA is the matrix obtained by multiplying each element of A by k; the (i, j)-th element of kA is k a_ij. The negative of a matrix is -A = (-1)A, and the difference A - B = A + (-1)B is computed entrywise. Scalar multiplication distributes: k(A + B) = kA + kB and (k + l)A = kA + lA.
keyMoves: Multiply every entry of A by the scalar k
Use -A = (-1)A for the negative
Compute A - B as A + (-1)B, entrywise
misconceptions: Multiplying only the diagonal or first row by k
Forgetting the difference subtracts corresponding entries

nodes.title: Multiply two matrices
nodes.brief: Multiply A by B when columns of A = rows of B, taking row-by-column dot products.
nodes.quote: The *product* of two matrices A and B is *defined* if the number of columns of A is equal to the number of rows of B.
nodes.explanation: The product AB is defined only if the number of columns of A equals the number of rows of B. If A is m x n and B is n x p, then AB = C is m x p, and the (i, k)-th element c_ik is found by taking the i-th row of A and the k-th column of B, multiplying element-wise and summing: c_ik = sum over j of a_ij b_jk. To get each entry you dot a row of A with a column of B.
keyMoves: Check columns of A equal rows of B before multiplying
For each (i, k) entry, dot row i of A with column k of B
Read off the product order as (rows of A) x (columns of B)
misconceptions: Multiplying entrywise like addition instead of row-by-column
Attempting AB when the inner dimensions do not match

nodes.title: Recognise that matrix multiplication is not commutative
nodes.brief: Understand that AB need not equal BA, and AB may be defined while BA is not.
nodes.quote: Thus matrix multiplication is not commutative.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The main fact: AB does not equal BA in general.
Matrix multiplication is NOT commutative. Even when AB and BA are both defined and have the same order, they will usually differ. Never assume AB = BA unless you have a specific reason (e.g. one factor is a scalar matrix).

PART 2 — Definedness is separate for each order.
If A is m×n and B is n×p, then AB is m×p (defined). But BA requires B to be p×n and A to be n×m — completely different size conditions. So AB being defined tells you nothing about whether BA is defined.

PART 3 — What DOES hold: associativity and distributivity.
Matrix multiplication IS associative: (AB)C = A(BC). It IS distributive over addition: A(B+C) = AB+AC. These laws are safe to use; commutativity is not.
keyMoves: Do not assume AB = BA
Check separately whether each of AB and BA is even defined
Use associativity (AB)C = A(BC) and distributivity, which DO hold
misconceptions: Assuming AB = BA as for ordinary numbers
Thinking that if AB is defined then BA must be too

nodes.title: Form the transpose of a matrix
nodes.brief: Form A' (or A^T) by interchanging the rows and columns of A.
nodes.quote: the matrix obtained by interchanging the rows and columns of A is called the transpose of A.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Form the transpose by swapping rows and columns.
The transpose of A, written A^T or A', is obtained by turning each row of A into a column (and each column into a row). If A is m×n, then A^T is n×m. The (i,j) entry of A^T equals the (j,i) entry of A.

PART 2 — Properties: double-transpose and linearity.
(A^T)^T = A (transposing twice returns the original). (A + B)^T = A^T + B^T and (kA)^T = kA^T, so transpose distributes over addition and scalar multiplication.

PART 3 — The reversal rule for products: (AB)^T = B^T A^T.
When transposing a product, the order of factors reverses. Writing A^T B^T instead of B^T A^T is the most common error with this operation.
keyMoves: Turn each row of A into the corresponding column of A'
Swap the order: an m x n matrix transposes to n x m
Apply (AB)' = B'A' with the factors reversed
misconceptions: Writing (AB)' = A'B' instead of B'A'
Keeping the same order m x n for the transpose

nodes.title: Recognise a symmetric matrix
nodes.brief: Identify a symmetric matrix as a square matrix with A' = A.
nodes.quote: A square matrix  $A = [a_{ij}]$  is said to be *symmetric* if A' = A
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition: A^T = A means entries mirror across the main diagonal.
A square matrix A is symmetric if A^T = A, meaning a_ji = a_ij for every (i, j). The matrix equals its own transpose — off-diagonal entries reflect across the main diagonal without any sign change.

PART 2 — Verify symmetry and extract the symmetric part.
To check: compute A^T and verify A^T = A. For any square matrix A, the expression (1/2)(A + A^T) is always symmetric. This gives the symmetric part of A.

PART 3 — Distinguish from skew-symmetric (A^T = -A).
Symmetric: A^T = A (entries mirror, no sign change; diagonal can be anything). Skew-symmetric: A^T = -A (off-diagonal entries flip sign; diagonal must be zero). Any square matrix = symmetric part + skew-symmetric part.
keyMoves: Confirm the matrix is square
Check a_ij = a_ji for all entries (A' = A)
Use (1/2)(A + A') to extract the symmetric part of any square matrix
misconceptions: Testing symmetry on a non-square matrix
Confusing symmetric (A' = A) with skew-symmetric (A' = -A)

nodes.title: Recognise a skew-symmetric matrix
nodes.brief: Identify a skew-symmetric matrix as a square matrix with A' = -A and zero diagonal.
nodes.quote: A square matrix  $A = [a_{ij}]$  is said to be *skew symmetric* matrix if A' = -A
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition: A^T = −A forces zero diagonal.
A square matrix A is skew-symmetric if A^T = −A, meaning a_ji = −a_ij for every (i, j). Setting i=j gives a_ii = −a_ii, so all diagonal entries must be zero.

PART 2 — Verify and contrast with symmetric.
To check skew-symmetry: compute A^T and verify A^T = −A (each off-diagonal entry must flip sign). Symmetric satisfies A^T = A (no sign flip). A zero matrix satisfies both simultaneously.

PART 3 — Extract the skew-symmetric part of any square matrix.
For any square matrix A, the expression (1/2)(A − A^T) is always skew-symmetric. This allows any square matrix to be decomposed as the sum of a symmetric part (1/2)(A + A^T) and a skew-symmetric part (1/2)(A − A^T).
keyMoves: Confirm the matrix is square
Check a_ji = -a_ij (A' = -A); the diagonal must be all zeros
Use (1/2)(A - A') to extract the skew-symmetric part of any square matrix
misconceptions: Allowing non-zero diagonal entries in a skew-symmetric matrix
Mixing up the sign condition with symmetry (A' = A)

nodes.title: Define an invertible matrix and its inverse
nodes.brief: Recognise an invertible square matrix A as one with a square B of the same order satisfying AB = BA = I; B = A-inverse is unique.
nodes.quote: If A is a square matrix of order *m*, and if there exists another square matrix B of the same order *m*, such that AB = BA = I, then B is called the *inverse* matrix of A
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Definition: AB = BA = I identifies the inverse.
A square matrix A is invertible if there exists a square matrix B of the same order such that AB = BA = I. We write B = A^(-1). Only square matrices can be invertible — the products must be defined and both equal I.

PART 2 — Verify BOTH products; the inverse is unique.
Checking only AB = I is not enough; you must also confirm BA = I. When both conditions hold, B is uniquely determined as A^(-1): no square matrix can have two different inverses.

PART 3 — Inverse of a product reverses order: (AB)^(-1) = B^(-1) A^(-1).
Exactly like the transpose reversal rule, inverting a product of invertible matrices reverses the factors. Proof: (AB)(B^(-1) A^(-1)) = A(BB^(-1))A^(-1) = AIA^(-1) = I, and the other product also gives I.
keyMoves: Require A and B to be square of the same order
Verify BOTH AB = I and BA = I to confirm B = A-inverse
Apply uniqueness and (AB)-inverse = B-inverse A-inverse
misconceptions: Checking only AB = I and not BA = I
Expecting a rectangular (non-square) matrix to have an inverse
